techniques.evidence_quote: The most common event that leads to a ransomware event that targets data in Amazon S3... is unintended disclosure of Identity and Access Management (IAM) access keys.
unintended disclosure of Identity and Access Management (IAM) access keys

techniques.technique_name: Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API
techniques.evidence_quote: an unauthorized user might be able to use AWS Security Token Service (AWS STS) session keys from the IAM instance profile for your EC2 instance... the instance is using Instance Metadata Service Version 1 (IMDSv1)
you might see an increase in API actions such as s3:ListBuckets, s3:GetBucketLocation, s3:GetBucketPolicy, and s3:GetBucketAcl
they use AWS API actions that they iterate through to discover the type of access that the exposed IAM principal has been granted

use the sum of the BytesDownloaded metric to gain insight into abnormal transfer spikes... region-DataTransfer-Out-Bytes metric, which shows the amount of data transferred from Amazon S3 to the internet
often the objects stored in S3 buckets are deleted, without the bad actor making copies. This is more like a data destruction event
ransomware event where objects are encrypted... a ransom demand is made for the safe return of these digital assets
Ransomware is a type of malware that bad actors can use to extort money from entities

attack_chain.description: Bad actor obtains unintentionally disclosed static IAM access keys (the most common initial vector). Alternative vector: exploiting an app with a software flaw on EC2 with IMDSv1 to retrieve STS session keys from the instance profile.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: unintended disclosure of Identity and Access Management (IAM) access keys

attack_chain.description: Attacker authenticates to AWS using the compromised IAM credentials.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: After a bad actor has obtained credentials, they use AWS API actions

evidence_chunks.quote: After a bad actor has obtained credentials, they use AWS API actions
After a bad actor has obtained credentials, they use AWS API actions

attack_chain.description: Attacker iterates through AWS API actions to enumerate the permissions granted to the compromised IAM principal, often generating error-producing API calls.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: they use AWS API actions that they iterate through to discover the type of access that the exposed IAM principal has been granted

attack_chain.description: Attacker enumerates S3 buckets and their configuration/policies via API calls such as s3:ListBuckets, s3:GetBucketLocation, s3:GetBucketPolicy, and s3:GetBucketAcl.
Attacker downloads/exfiltrates S3 objects, which can show up as abnormal BytesDownloaded spikes and region-DataTransfer-Out-Bytes increases.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: use the sum of the BytesDownloaded metric to gain insight into abnormal transfer spikes

evidence_chunks.quote: use the sum of the BytesDownloaded metric to gain insight into abnormal transfer spikes
use the sum of the BytesDownloaded metric to gain insight into abnormal transfer spikes

attack_chain.description: Attacker deletes S3 objects (often without retaining copies), making the event effectively a data destruction/ransomware event.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: often the objects stored in S3 buckets are deleted, without the bad actor making copies. This is more like a data destruction event

evidence_chunks.quote: often the objects stored in S3 buckets are deleted, without the bad actor making copies. This is more like a data destruction event
often the objects stored in S3 buckets are deleted, without the bad actor making copies. This is more like a data destruction event

attack_chain.description: Alternatively, attacker encrypts S3 objects and demands a ransom for the encryption key / safe return of the data.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a ransomware event where objects are encrypted... a ransom demand is made for the safe return of these digital assets

attack_chain.description: Attacker extorts the victim for ransom payment, sometimes demanding a second ransom not to leak exfiltrated copies.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: bad actors who have a copy of the data demand a second ransom—promising not to retain the data

evidence_chunks.quote: bad actors who have a copy of the data demand a second ransom—promising not to retain the data
bad actors who have a copy of the data demand a second ransom—promising not to retain the data

initial_access_vector: Unintended disclosure of static IAM access keys (most common); alternatively, SSRF/application flaw on an EC2 instance using IMDSv1 to retrieve STS session credentials from the attached instance profile.
persistence_mechanism: Potentially creating additional IAM users, access keys, or roles for persistent access (implied by guidance to review CloudTrail for persistent access created by the bad actor).
privilege_escalation: Not explicitly described; attacker leverages whatever permissions the compromised IAM principal (user or instance profile) was granted.
